hypothesis: Combining Bollinger band breakouts with ATR volatility filtering and RSI-based mean reversion captures strong trend moves in high‑volatility periods while exploiting pullbacks in low‑volatility regimes, reducing false entries in ranging markets.

When ATR indicates a high volatility regime, the price is more likely to sustain a breakout above the supertrend line, so a breakout entry is favored; in low volatility regimes the market tends to oscillate around the VWAP, so a mean‑reversion entry against the VWAP is more appropriate. ATR‑based stop‑loss and take‑profit automatically scale risk with market volatility, improving risk‑reward in both regimes.

Combining a trend confirmation with the SuperTrend, a mean‑reversion trigger around VWAP in low volatility regimes, and an RSI filter to avoid entering overbought or oversold conditions reduces false signals and captures both strong trends and pullbacks, improving Sharpe while keeping drawdowns manageable.

Using ATR as a volatility switch, the strategy takes trend‑following breakouts when volatility is high and reverts to a mean‑reversion entry against the VWAP when volatility is low, while the SuperTrend confirms the prevailing trend direction. This dual regime reduces false signals in ranging periods and captures strong trend moves in volatile moments, improving risk‑adjusted performance and limiting drawdown.

Using ATR to distinguish high‑volatility breakouts from low‑volatility mean reversion, Supertrend to confirm trend direction, and VWAP as a dynamic support/resistance in quiet periods reduces false entries and captures profitable moves in both regimes.

When volatility is high (ATR above a threshold) the market tends to break out of the supertrend trend line, so a breakout entry captures momentum; when volatility is low, price tends to revert to the VWAP mean, so a mean‑reversion entry relative to VWAP in the current supertrend direction reduces false signals.
